questions.question: List the three main steps of one iteration of the standard K-Means algorithm after initialization.
expectedPoints: Assignment Step: Assign each data point to the cluster whose centroid is closest (usually based on Euclidean distance).
Update Step: Recalculate the centroids for each cluster as the mean of all points assigned to that cluster.
Check Convergence: Repeat the assignment and update steps until centroids stabilize or a stopping criterion is met.

questions.question: Discuss the major limitations of the K-Means clustering algorithm. How does the K-Means++ algorithm address one of these limitations?
expectedPoints: Sensitivity to Initial Centroids: Results can vary based on random initial centroid placement, leading to local minima.
Choice of k: Requires the user to pre-specify the number of clusters, which is often unknown.
Cluster Shape Bias: Tends to produce spherical clusters and performs poorly on clusters of other shapes.
Sensitivity to Outliers: Centroids (means) are heavily influenced by extreme values.

K-Means++ Addresses Initialization: It provides a smarter initialization method by choosing initial centroids that are far apart, reducing the chance of poor convergence and leading to better overall results.
